feed.subtitle: SAP Integration Suite blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: Integration Suite migrations are not hard because of the technology, but ...
entry.content:
The technical migration itself is honestly the easiest part most of the time. The real challenge in Integration Suite migrations is everything around coordination, communication, dependencies, stakeholder alignment, and managing expectations.

Yes, integration developers can usually work through technical blockers. There’s already a solid amount of documentation, guides, and community knowledge available to help solve those. But where projects truly struggle is in the non-technical execution layer.

Where Things Actually Break Down?

From experience, the biggest pain points are not in development, but in orchestration:

  • Coordinating with third parties
  • Asking the right migration and modernization questions
  • Managing endpoint switch discussions
  • Convincing clients to move away from legacy patterns
  • Handling RACI / RAID properly
  • Aligning cutover timelines across systems
  • Tracking cross-team dependencies
  • Managing parallel S/4HANA changes alongside integration migration

These are the areas where delays quietly accumulate and often go unnoticed until they become critical.

The Hidden Trap: “No Code Change Required”.

One of the most underestimated risks in these migrations is the assumption:

“There’s no code change required from third parties, so this should be straightforward.”

In reality, this creates ambiguity and hidden gaps:

  • Ownership becomes unclear
  • Communication is minimized or deprioritized
  • Validation cycles are delayed
  • Endpoint changes get underestimated
  • Dependencies remain undocumented

And that’s exactly how migration timelines start slipping, without obvious technical blockers.

The Real Skill Gap!

Most integration developers can figure out:

One of the most underestimated risks in these migrations is the assumption:

✅iFlows  ✅ Mappings  ✅ Adapters ✅ Connectivity

But many struggle with:

❌Stakeholder management ❌ Cross-system coordination ❌ Migration governance ❌ Dependency tracking ❌ Structured planning

This gap is rarely addressed, yet it’s the single biggest driver of project risk.

What’s Missing Today?

There’s a clear need for structured, practical migration playbooks that go beyond just technical steps. Not more documentation on how to build an iFlow, but real-world guidance on how to run a migration successfully.

 

What a Good Migration Playbook Should Cover?

1. Pre-Migration Preparation

  • What an Integration Suite developer should assess upfront
  • Inventory of artifacts and landscape dependencies
  • Interface criticality and usage patterns
  • Business impact assessment

2. Minimum PO/ non-SAP Middleware Knowledge Required

Not every Integration Suite developer needs deep PO/ non-SAP Middleware expertise, but they must understand:

  • How to access and interpret existing PO/ non-SAP Middleware objects
  • Interface types, mappings, and adapter configurations
  • Scheduling, triggers, payloads and dependencies

 

3. Stakeholder Mapping

  • Who owns the interface? (Business vs Technical)
  • Who owns endpoints?
  • Who validates?
  • Who signs off?

Without this clarity, everything slows down.

4. Key Coordination Checkpoints

  • Design validation
  • Endpoint confirmation
  • Connectivity readiness
  • Test cycle alignment
  • Cutover readiness

These should be planned, not discovered mid-project.

5. Migration & Cutover Strategy

  • Big bang vs phased approach
  • Parallel run considerations
  • Rollback planning
  • Freeze windows and deployment coordination

6. Modernization Opportunities

Migration should not be a lift-and-shift exercise.

This is the moment to:

  • Eliminate legacy patterns
  • Simplify integrations
  • Standardize error handling
  • Improve monitoring and observability

7. Transport & Release Dependencies

  • Cross-system release alignment
  • Dependency tracking across teams
  • Change management workflows

A missed dependency can delay an entire cutover.

8. Governance During Execution

  • Clear RACI model (this one is my favourite)
  • RAID (Risks, Assumptions, Issues, Dependencies) tracking
  • Regular sync points
  • Escalation paths

Governance is what keeps everything moving when complexity increases.

Final thoughts!

PO → Integration Suite migrations don’t fail because the technology is too complex. They fail because:

  • Communication is unclear
  • Ownership is ambiguous
  • Dependencies are underestimated
  • Planning is incomplete

The real challenge is orchestration, not implementation.

If we invest more in:

  • Structured migration approaches
  • Clear coordination models
  • Practical playbooks

We can significantly improve delivery success, without needing to reinvent the technical wheel.

Because in the end, it’s not about whether you can migrate— it’s about whether you can migrate smoothly, predictably, and successfully.

entry.title: SAP CPI WorkMate — Chrome Extension to View Data Store and JMS Payloads Directly
entry.content:
You are debugging an integration flow and want to view a message from a Data Store or a JMS Queue to see what's inside the payload? Here's what that currently looks like:

  1. Open Monitor → Data Stores (or Message Queues)
  2. Find the entry
  3. Downalod it as ZIP file
  4. Extract the Zip
  5. Open the files in a text editor of your choice
  6. Manually format the JSON or XML to make it readable

That's six steps — just to read a message payload. Imagine doing this multipe times a Day!!

Introducing SAP CPI WorkMate

SAP CPI WorkMate is a free Chrome extension that cuts all of that down to a fewer click.

It injects a View Data button directly into the SAP Integration Suite monitoring pages — both Data Stores and JMS Message Queues. You select a row, click the button, and the payload opens instantly in a built-in viewer right in your browser.

   The extension uses your existing browser session, so there's no separate login or token to manage.

What You Get

Instant payload viewing
Click any entry row in Data Stores or any message in JMS Queues, hit View Data, and the payload is fetched, unzipped, and displayed — all in the browser.

Syntax highlighting
JSON and XML payloads are colour-coded out of the box, making it easy to scan large messages at a glance.

Auto Format
Turn on Auto Format in settings and payloads are automatically pretty-printed the moment they load. Or keep it off and format manually when you need it.

Full-text search
Use the search bar inside the viewer to find a specific field or value across the payload. Navigate between matches with Prev / Next.

Line numbers
Every file shows line numbers alongside the content — handy when you're referencing a specific line in a large XML document.

Multiple files in one view
Data Store or JMS payloads often contain more than one file inside the zip (body, headers, properties). The viewer shows all of them in a left sidebar — click any file to switch.

Copy and Download
Copy the current file to clipboard with one click, or download it directly to your machine.

Configurable appearance
Choose between Dark and Light themes, three overlay sizes, and three font sizes — all saved across sessions via the extension settings popup.

How to Install

Find SAP CPI Workmate in Chrome Web Store and click Add to Chrome.

How to Use

For Data Stores:
1. Navigate to Monitor → Data Stores
2. Click a data store in the left panel
3. Click any entry row to select it
4. Click the View Data button that appears next to the Filter by ID field
5. Browse the payload files in the left sidebar of the viewer

For JMS Message Queues:
1. Navigate to Monitor → Message Queues
2. Click a queue in the left panel
3. Click any message row to select it
4. Click View Data

The View Data button is automatically greyed out when no row is selected, and resets when you switch to a different data store or queue — so you always know you're viewing the right payload.

A Note on Security

The extension does not collect, store, or transmit any data outside your browser. All API calls go directly to your *.hana.ondemand.com tenant using your existing browser session. Nothing is sent to any third-party server.

Links

Chrome Web Store: SAP CPI Workmate
GitHub: SAP-CPI-Workmate
License: MIT — free to use, modify, and distribute

If you run into any issues or have suggestions for features, feel free to leave a comment below or open an issue on GitHub.

entry.title: Explore SAP Adapters using Discovery Missions
entry.content:
Ever feel like there are plenty of posts about your SAP Adapter yet nothing like a step by step, guided tutorial that allows you to explore the adapter in action. While SAP Blogs and Technology posts generally provide enough to address questions regarding  basic configurations and troubleshooting, if you feel these posts are inadequate, you might be looking in the wrong place.

At the heart of SAP Discovery Center are Missions which are a curated, hands-on journey experience that allow you to build and explore your integration scenarios block by block.

What is SAP Discovery Center?

The SAP Discovery Center is SAP's official learning and enablement hub for SAP Business Technology Platform. To browse missions hosted on SAP you only need a free SAP Universal ID. 

Using Discovery Missions 

Unlike generic tutorials, every mission is scoped to a specific outcome. I was able to find specific missions for SAP S/4HANA to a third-party system using SAP Integration Suite.

  • Each mission has a graphical board  which has dedicated sections for pre-requisites, authentication setups, and detailed configurations that allow you to get a modular understanding of the entire mission.
  • You can mark a section as complete and it will allow you to view your progress on the dashboard. Really handy for someone like me who gets overwhelmed if an integration use case takes too long !

Why Missions work : User Oriented Tutorials

Every Discovery Mission is user oriented. This means you can pick up any mission and start exploring it at random and you will be guided through every step of the mission setup. What you can expect in a mission:

  • A single, measurable outcome defined before step one. No ambiguity about when you are done.
  • Each tile in your project board contains no more than a few steps related to the tile header.
  • There are screenshots which help you follow along, giving you the confidence to progress.

Need Further Support?

  • Discovery Missions allow you to connect with others, who have tried similar missions.
  • Many Missions are fully guided, meaning you can drop a comment to the Product Expert who will help you in case you get stuck.

Best Practices

  • Open BAH alongside the Mission: The SAP Business Accelerator Hub  is a great aid while navigating through adapter missions. When a step says "Configure the Jira adapter," having the adapter's official API spec open in a second tab makes parameter choices instantly clear.
  • Take notes on credentials and IDs: Missions often ask you to copy a Client ID or Tenant URL that you created in Step 2. Keep a scratch doc open. Nothing derails a mission faster than hunting for an OAuth secret you generated 45 minutes ago.
  • Join the SAP Community thread for the Mission: Every mission links to a Community discussion thread. If you get stuck, someone else almost certainly hit the same wall and the answer is already there, often from the mission author themselves.

entry.title: Managing JMS Queues Programmatically with the new SAP Cloud Integration JMS OData API
entry.content:
If you operate integration scenarios on SAP Cloud Integration capability of SAP Integration Suite and rely on JMS messaging, you have likely needed to inspect messages, move messages to a different queue, or trigger a retry — all without opening the monitoring UI. The new JMS OData API makes all of that possible.

This post walks through the new public OData API MessagingQueues / MessagingMessages : what it covers, how to use it, and the details you need to get it right.

This new JMS OData API is available starting with SAP Cloud Integration version 7.52.x for the Cloud Foundry runtime and version 8.41 for the Edge Integration Cell runtime.

What the API Enables

The API exposes two entities: MessagingQueues and MessagingMessages. Together, they give you control over the JMS layer of your tenant.

Queue operations let you list all queues with their capacity metrics, inspect a single queue, and delete a queue when it is no longer needed.

Message operations let you list messages with filters, retrieve a single message by its composite key, download the raw payload bytes, delete individual messages, move messages between queues, and retry failed messages.

Getting Started

You need a Process Integration Runtime service instance with the integration-flow plan and the OAuth credentials from its service binding. All endpoints live under /api/v1/ on your tenant URL, and authentication uses OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials. The base URL for all requests:

https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/

A minimal workflow for programmatic error handling:

  1. GET /MessagingQueues to find all queues.
  2. GET /MessagingQueues('{queueName}')/MessagingMessages with filters scoped to your scenario (sender, correlationId, time range, etc.).
  3. Inspect the payload via /$value on interesting messages.
  4. POST /RetryMessagingMessages to retry failed messages, or POST /MoveMessagingMessages to redirect messages to a different queue.

The next sections cover each of these endpoints in detail.

API Endpoints

Listing Queues

GET https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/MessagingQueues

Each queue in the response looks like this:

{
  "d": {
    "results": [
      {
        "queueName": "orders-inbound",
        "numberOfMessages": 123,
        "active": true,
        "exclusive": false,
        "MessagingMessages": {
          "__deferred": {
            "uri": "https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/MessagingQueues('orders-inbound')/MessagingMessages"
          }
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

To get the total number of queues without fetching the full list:

GET https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/MessagingQueues/$count

This returns a plain-text integer.

The queue feed itself has no row cap — all queues configured for the tenant are returned in a single response.

Listing and Filtering Messages

GET https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/MessagingQueues('orders-inbound')/MessagingMessages
    ?sender=OrderSystem
    &receiver=SAP-ERP
    &createdAfter=1771900000000
    &createdBefore=1771986400000

The API supports the following filter parameters — all optional, all AND-combined:

ParameterDescription
jmsMessageIdFilter by JMS message ID
correlationIdFilter by correlation ID
senderFilter by sender
receiverFilter by receiver
mplIdFilter by Message Processing Log ID
messageTypeFilter by message type
applicationIdFilter by application ID
createdAfter / createdBeforeTime bounds on SAP_CreatedTime (epoch ms UTC)
pageSizeAmount of entries to return

There is no $filter, $orderby, $select, $expand, or $search — these return 400 if supplied.

$top, $skip, $inlinecount, and $skiptoken are silently ignored (the server does not page on them; row count is governed solely by pageSize). $format is accepted. Unknown $* options return 400.

How Message Listing Works (no pagination)

The API does not support $top or $skip — both are silently ignored. Instead, the server applies a fixed row cap of 100 rows. You can override the default row cap per request with pageSize in the range 100–10,000.

The reason: message listing uses JMS browse semantics, not a random-access table. Queues change continuously — messages are consumed, expired, and reordered between requests. Offset paging ($skip=500) would scan the broker on every call, resulting in unstable results.

The supported approach for large datasets is time-window slicing:

# Window 1: Monday morning
GET …/MessagingMessages?sender=ACME&createdAfter=1777000000000&createdBefore=1777043200000

# Window 2: Monday afternoon
GET …/MessagingMessages?sender=ACME&createdAfter=1777043200000&createdBefore=1777086400000

If a window still hits the row cap, narrow it further by using a shorter time range or additional filters.

Getting a Single Message and its Payload

The composite key (jmsMessageId, queueName) can be used to get a single message:

GET https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/MessagingMessages(
  jmsMessageId='x-hex-3333...43864',
  queueName='orders-inbound'
)

To download the raw payload bytes of a message, append /$value:

GET https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/MessagingMessages(
  jmsMessageId='x-hex-3333...43864',
  queueName='orders-inbound'
)/$value

The server streams the payload directly from the broker and decompresses transparently if the message was stored compressed.

An empty payload returns 200 with Content-Length: 0.

A top-level GET /MessagingMessages (without a queue context) returns 404. If too many concurrent payload downloads are active on the instance, the server returns a 503 (SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE) error.

Deleting a Message

DELETE https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/MessagingMessages(
  jmsMessageId='x-hex-3333...43864',
  queueName='orders-inbound'
)

Response: 200 OK with a confirmation body:

{
 "operation": "DELETE",
 "processedCount": 1 
}

This is permanent and irreversible.

Deleting a Queue

DELETE https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/MessagingQueues('orders-inbound')

By default, the queue must be empty.

Pass forceDelete=true to delete and discard any remaining messages in the queue:

DELETE https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/MessagingQueues('orders-inbound')?forceDelete=true

Response on success: 200 OK with { "operation": "DELETE", "processedCount": 1 }.

If the queue still has messages and forceDelete is not true, the server returns 409 (QUEUE_NOT_EMPTY). If deployed integration flows still reference the queue, the server returns a 400 (QUEUE_IN_USE) error.

Moving Messages Between Queues

Move operations use POST /MoveMessagingMessages with a JSON body. Selection is mutually exclusive: whole queue, specific message IDs, or filter-based.

Move Entire Queue

POST https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/MoveMessagingMessages
Header: 
- Content-Type: application/json
Body:
{
  "sourceQueue": "orders-error",
  "targetQueue": "orders-inbound"
}

Move Specific Messages

POST https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/MoveMessagingMessages
Header:
- Content-Type: application/json
Body:
{
  "sourceQueue": "orders-error",
  "targetQueue": "orders-inbound",
  "jmsMessageIds": ["x-hex-3333...43864", "x-hex-4444...43865"]
}

Move by Filter

POST https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/MoveMessagingMessages
Header:
- Content-Type: application/json
Body:
{
  "sourceQueue": "orders-error",
  "targetQueue": "orders-inbound",
  "correlationId": "abc123",
  "createdAfter": "1771900000000",
  "createdBefore": "1771986400000"
}

Response: 200 OK with { "operation": "MOVE", "processedCount": 42 }. For ID- or filter-based selection, processedCount is the number of messages actually moved. For a whole queue move, it reflects the queue's numberOfMessages at dispatch time, not a recount after the operation.

Unknown body fields return a 400 (INVALID_INPUT). The source and target queue must differ. Missing source or target queue returns a 404 (NOT_FOUND) error.

Retrying Failed Messages

Retry operations use POST /RetryMessagingMessages. The same selection options apply: whole queue, single message, message IDs, or filters.

Retry Entire Queue

POST https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/RetryMessagingMessages
Header:
- Content-Type: application/json
Body:
{ 
  "queueName": "orders-inbound"
}

Retry a Single Message

POST https://{tenant-url}/api/v1/RetryMessagingMessages
Header:
- Content-Type: application/json
Body:
{
  "queueName": "orders-inbound",
  "jmsMessageId": "x-hex-3333...43864"
}

Response: 200 OK with { "operation": "RETRY", "processedCount": 1 }. The same reporting rules from move operations apply: actual count for ID/filter selection, numberOfMessages at dispatch time for whole-queue retry.

Retrying on an stopped queue returns 400 (INVALID_INPUT). If the message does not exist, it returns a 404 (NOT_FOUND) response.

Technical Details Every Consumer Should Know

The Composite Key

Every MessagingMessage is identified by two components: jmsMessageId (the JMS Message ID) and queueName (the queue it lives in).

Timestamp Convention

All timestamp values in response properties (createdAt, overdueAt, expirationDate, nextRetry) and in filter parameters (createdAfter, createdBefore) — are epoch milliseconds UTC, represented as a 13-digit integer. For example, 1771933250829 is 2026-03-25T06:40:50.829Z.

OAuth 2.0 Scopes

The API uses OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials. Obtain a token from your tenant's Process Integration Runtime service instance.

Role (NEO)Role-Templates (Cloud FoundryOperation
ESBDataStore.readDataStoresAndQueuesReadAll GET operations (queues, messages, artifacts)
ESBDataStore.readPayloadDataStorePayloadsReadPayload download via GET /$value
ESBDataStore.deleteDataStoresAndQueuesDeleteDELETE queue, DELETE message
ESBDataStore.retryQueuesRetryPOST /MoveMessagingMessages
POST /RetryMessagingMessages
ESBDataStore.ActivateQueuesActivateActivate / deactivate queue function imports

Audit Logging

Successful deletes, moves, retries, and payload downloads are recorded in the tenant audit log. Each entry captures who triggered the call, what was done, and the main details such as — queue names, target queue on move, whether forceDelete was used, and the processedCount on move and retry. Ordinary reads of queue or message metadata are not audit-logged. Only payload download (/$value) is treated as sensitive read access.

Error Responses

All API-layer errors use a consistent OData envelope:

{
  "error": {
    "code": "QUEUE_NOT_EMPTY",
    "message": {
      "lang": "en",
      "value": "Queue 'orders-inbound' contains messages. Pass forceDelete=true to delete anyway."
    }
  }
}

Error codes you will encounter on this surface: BAD_REQUEST, INVALID_FILTER, INVALID_INPUT, NOT_FOUND, QUEUE_IN_USE, QUEUE_NOT_EMPTY, QUEUE_NOT_DELETABLE, SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE, INTERNAL_ERROR.

Note that 401, 403, and 429 responses come from the surrounding runtime platform. Their shapes may not conform to the OData envelope above.

Share your Experience

Have you built operational tooling on top of the JMS OData API? We'd like to hear what patterns you've found useful.

entry.title: Clean Core by Design: Event Enablement Cockpit as a Core Integration Enabler in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Enterprise Event Enablement is one of the core components of the modern SAP Enterprise Integration Architecture for SAP S/4 HANA, which enables seamless exchange of business events across different platforms for Event-Driven communication. Events from SAP S/4 can be sent to SAP BTP Integration Suite Event Mesh/ AEM/ SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, where they can be consumed by any recipient based on the requirement.
To exchange events between SAP S/4 and  BTP EM, a communication connection setup is required. This connection is maintained by the Enterprise Event Enablement framework, which enables the data flow from the SAP S/4 HANA to the Business Event Consumer, and BTP services play it's role as the message broker(Integration Suite EM/ AEM).

A channel represents a single connection to a service instance of the SAP BTP, Event Mesh. For more information, see Creating, Configuring and Managing Channels.

Note: Channel configurations can be done via SAP Event Mesh, SAP Advance Event Mesh or SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, depending on your scenario and architecture, in this blog we will be going through SAP EM. For SAP ECC, you need ASAPIO for Events Configuration/ Enablement because Standard Business Events, like they are natively available in S/4 are not available in ECC.



SAP S/4 Event Producers

Non SAP S/4 Event Producers

Create a Destination through the SM59 transaction and setup the OAuth 2.0 configuration on the S/4 HANA system, both must be configured as per the service key instance of the corresponding event mesh instance.
After the channel creation is completed, you can list which events can be part of this channel, and an outbound binding needs to be setup to publish the events of an S/4 HANA system.
All administration, configuration, and monitoring steps are available in transaction SPRO under the Enterprise Event Enablement Node,

Under the Enterprise Event Enablement framework, below are the authorization objects and roles that are available,
Under the Hood of Enterprise Event Enablement Framework, the available authorization objects and roles are below,

Authorization Objects: /IWXBE/ADM, /IWXBE/USR, /IWXBE/SUP

Roles: SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_ADM, SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_BUSI, SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_MDT

Creating, Configuring and Managing Channels

  1. Create an EM Service Instance
  2. Creating Channels using a Service Key
    1. Creating and Managing Channels
      1. Create an RFC Destination
      2. OAuth 2.0 Client Config
      3. Manage Channel and Parameters

1. Create an EM Service Instance
Create a service instance for the EM,

  1. Go to your SAP BTP cockpit global account and subaccount.
      - To see how the navigation works in the SAP BTP cockpit, refer to Navigate in the Cockpit.
  2. Expand Services  Instances and Subscriptions.
  3. Choose Create.
  4. Choose Event Mesh and the default service plan.


  5. Choose your Cloud Foundry space, enter an instance name, and choose Next.

  6. Specify parameters using a JSON file for the Service Key.
    Example:
{
  "emname": "<emname>",
  "namespace": "<namespace e.g. a/b/c>",
  "version": "1.1.0",
  "options": {
    "management": true,
    "messagingrest": true,
    "messaging": true
  },
  "rules": {
    "queueRules": {
        "publishFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ],
        "subscribeFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ]
    },
    "topicRules": {
        "publishFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ],
        "subscribeFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ]
    }
  },
	"xs-security": { //optional//
     "oauth2-configuration": { 
      "credential-types": [
        "binding-secret",
        "x509"
      ]
    }
  }
}

2. Creating Channels using a Service Key
Once the service instance key is created, it will have all the necessary information to make a connection, it will be used in the automatic process creation and configuration of the destination and OAuth 2.0 config, which will be handled by the Event Enablement Framework itself.

To create a channel using a service key, proceed as follows:
1. Execute the Event Enablement Configuration.
2. Press the via Service Key (Create new channel via Service Key) button.


3. Enter the following details:
    - a. Channel
    - b. Description
    - c. Destination (Enter your preferred RFC destination name)
    - d. OAuth 2.0 Configuration (Enter your preferred name. The name must not have been used yet)
4. Enter the service key.

5. Choose Save configuration or press Enter.

Choose Save configuration or press Enter.

2.i. Creating and Managing Channels

2.i.a. Creating RFC destination,
Steps:
1. Go to BTP -> Event Mesh -> Cloud Foundry Environment, to get the certificates, follow the steps below:
     - Launch the token endpoint URL, https://<enterprise messaging url>/oauth/token that you receive from the service instance of SAP EM.
     - Export all certificates from the certificates path.
2. Logon to the SAP S/4HANA, on-premise system that has the SAP_GWFND component.
3. Open the transaction STRUST, select the required node SSL Client (Standard) and import all the certificates.
4. Open the transaction SMICM, select Administration ICM Exit Soft Local to clear the cache.
5. In SPRO, open SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide SAP NetWeaver Enterprise Event Enablement Administration Channel Connection Settings.
6. Open Manage RFC Destination.
7. Create the RFC destination, give name and description and select the connection type as G and Save.
8. Under Technical Settings, in Target System Setting, in the Host field, enter the domain name of the <app-url> given in the URI, example, wss://<app-url>/protocols/mqtt311ws of the service instance key in the mqtt311ws protocol.
9. (Optional) Under Technical Settings, in HTTP Proxy Settings, enter the proxy details.
10. Under the Logon and Security tab, in the Security option, enable SSL as Active and import the SSL Certificate from the certificate list and save.

2.i.b. OAuth 2.0 Client Config

Steps:

1. Inside SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide SAP NetWeaver Enterprise Event Enablement Administration Channel Connection Settings.
2. Open the Manage OAuth Client Setup and Click on Create.
3. In OAuth 2.0 Client Profile field, select the /IWXBE/MGW_MQTT profile and Enter a configuration name, and in Client ID, enter the EM service instance Client ID and press ok.
4. Under the Details section, choose the Administration and place the Client Secret you receive from the EM service instance.
5. Under Authorization Server Settings, enter the following:
   - In the Authorization Endpoint field, enter the token endpoint URL of the service instance and replace <token> with <authorize>.
   - For the Token Endpoint field, set the token endpoint URL of the service instance.
6. In the Access Settings, under the Resource Access Authentication section, choose Header Field.
7. Select the Grant Type as Client Credentials and enter the proxy settings(if applicable) and save.

 2.i.c. Manage Channel and Parameters

Steps:

1. Go to SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide  SAP NetWeaver  Enterprise Event Enablement  Administration  Channel Connection Settings  Manage Channels and Parameters or execute transaction.
2. Either create the new or select the already created one(based on the requirement).
3. Create/ Configure the output binding configuration,


4. In the Topic Name field, enter the namespace derived from the service key of the given instance(Topic space is the identifier for events that originate from the source)

5. Make the following entries in the Parameters section (mandatory).
- OAuth 2.0 Client Profile: /IWXBE/MGW_AMQP.
- OAuth 2.0 Configuration: Select the configuration name created in OAuth 2.0 Account Client Configuration.
6. Entries can be made in the optional parameters, below are the available parameters:
- Max Reconnect Attempts: Enterprise Event Enablement Framework executes to a defined number of times reestablish the connection in case of connection failure, the value of 0 or no value, the framework tries to connect until the connection is established. The channel is deactivated after reaching the reconnect attempts and still the connection is not established.
- Quality of Service (QoS): There are two values:
   - At Most Once(The message arrives at SAP BTP, EM mostly once)
   - At Least Once(QoS=1, default value if no value is entered).
     This quality of service ensures that the message arrives at SAP BTP, EM at least once)
- Reconnect Waittime:
- The Enterprise Event Enablement framework waits for the specified time(in seconds) and after that tries to reconnect. The framework is setup in such a way when the failure occurs, it increases the waittime till it reaches the Max Reconnect waittime(1800 seconds). The default reconnect waittime is 10 seconds, if the Max Reconnect Attempts value is not maintained, the framework tries to reconnect until the connection is established but the channel will not be deactivated during this period.
7. Choose the Save configuration button. or press Enter and select either Active<->Deactivate to activate or deactivate the channel.
- If the channel is active, only then will the events be sent. The channel might be in inactive status if the connection was terminated and reconnects failed.
- The channel will be deactivated during runtime, if the number of reconnect attempts are completed and the connection is still not established.
8. Press Check connection to perform a connection test.
9. If the connection test fails for an active channel, the corresponding error message is displayed. The same applies to an inactive channel.
 
 
Event Discovery Service Activation Step:
Once the Above steps are done, activate the Event Discovery Service and start Maintaining the Event Topics Accordingly,
Procedure

1. In transaction SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to Enterprise Event Enablement Administration.
2. Execute Activate Event Discovery Service and Choose Add Service, add alias as LOCAL and give External Service Name as BROWSER_SRV and then click on Get Services.
3. Select the service and Choose Add Selected Services, save it as local object and choose continue.
4. If the system alias is missing, go to System Aliases pane, choose Add System Alias and select new entries.
5. Enter the Service Doc Identifier as ZBROWSER_SRV_0001 and enter LOCAL in the SAP System Alias field.
6. Enable the Default System checkbox and click on save.
7. In the ICF Nodes pane, choose ICF Node and choose Activate.

 

 Configure the EM Client:

1. Message Clients
Goto EM Application and check the Message Clients( if case there is no, then create one otherwise you use the existing one)


2. Create Queue


3. Queue Subscription

4. Subscribe to the specific topics as per the requirement

5. Create a Webhook(Optional)
Use a webhook only when the consumer application does not support AMQP or MQTT protocols.

Conclusion



Now the setup is completed, try to test it through the test tab, and you are ready to enable the business events from the S/4 system. In this way, you will be able to set up the Event-Driven Architecture effectively within the S/4 system. Through this approach, you can set up a standardized and scalable way of exposing the Business Events in real time, enabling the flow of information through multiple ecosystems across the enterprise landscapes. By applying this approach, you can remove the reliance on the tightly coupled design and be more flexible through a loosely coupled design, which makes all the systems notified instantly.



References:
https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-cloud-application-event-hub/sap-cloud-application-event-hub-service-guide/integration-use-cases
https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-integration-connectivity/creating-and-managing-channels?version=s4_hana
https://help.sap.com/docs/BTP/65de2977205c403bbc107264b8eccf4b/0874895f1f78459f9517da55a11ffebd.html
https://asapio.com/docs/connectors/sap-aem/
https://asapio.com/docs/designer/?asl_highlight=clean&p_asid=1

entry.title: 2026年第一季度产品亮点—SAP 集成套件
entry.content:
本文为译文版本。请在此查看英文原文。

请注意：SAP Cloud ALM 和 Advanced Event Mesh 目前在中国暂不可用。

欢迎阅读 SAP Integration Suite 最新发布功能的又一季度回顾！2026 年第一季度的新能力聚焦于提升运营效率和改进事件驱动机制，旨在帮助每天构建、运行和故障排除集成的从业者更加顺畅地开展工作。欢迎浏览本次亮点，并查看相关文档和演示以获取操作细节，同时参考路线图了解完整功能列表。

增强版现已发布
SAP Integration Suite 增强版通过提供受管控的实时连接、低代码开发工具以及 AI 驱动的运营能力（如 Text-to-Flow、脚本优化、API 异常检测、API 流量预测，以及连接器、事件网格、Document AI、告警和传输管理），帮助将 AI 和智能代理 AI 从试点阶段推进到生产环境。它能够减少集成技术债务，在云和本地系统之间实现安全的智能代理操作，并创建可复用的构建模块，从而更快、更可重复地产生 AI 价值。

加速您的 AI 计划并使其具备企业级能力，详情请参阅 SAP Discovery Center。

事件驱动集成

在 Developer Hub 中发现并发布高级事件网格事件
内容管理员可以直接在 Developer Hub 中发现并发布 Advanced Event Mesh 事件，从而将事件以事件产品的形式对外提供，并帮助应用开发人员更轻松地搜索和找到相关事件。要使用该功能，需要创建一个目标（destination），将 SAP Integration Suite 的 Advanced Event Mesh 连接到 Developer Hub；连接完成后，相关业务系统将显示在 Developer Hub 中以供发现和发布。

更多详细说明，请参阅文档：在 Developer Hub 中发现并发布 Advanced Event Mesh 事件。

 

从 SAP Event Mesh 迁移到 SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh 的支持
此次更新提供了技术和流程方面的支持，帮助客户从 SAP Event Mesh（默认方案）迁移到 SAP Integration Suite 的 Advanced Event Mesh，使企业能够将其事件驱动架构迁移到更强大、功能更丰富的平台。该指南面向已在使用 SAP Event Mesh 默认方案的客户，概述了扩展其基于事件架构的步骤。阅读指南：从 SAP Event Mesh 迁移到 SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh。

流程集成

Edge Integration Cell 对 Valkey 的支持
SAP Integration Suite 中的 Edge Integration Cell 功能现在支持将 Valkey 作为 Redis 和 HANA 数据库之外的另一种数据存储选项。该选项可在安装时选择，支持 8.0–8.2 版本，使管理员可以在部署中选择 Valkey，从而帮助降低总体拥有成本。

有关步骤和前提条件，请参阅：部署 Edge Integration Cell 解决方案。

在 SAP Focused Run 和 SAP Cloud ALM 上监控消息处理
用户可以通过基于推送的方式，将日志发送至 SAP Focused Run（FRUN）和 SAP Cloud ALM（CALM），以监控来自 Edge Integration Cell 的消息处理。这使得在单一应用中集中监控多个 Edge Integration Cell 运行时成为可能，可以基于消息处理日志状态配置告警，并支持在离线场景下仍通过 SAP Focused Run 监控相关日志。

更多信息请参阅：在 SAP Focused Run 和 SAP Cloud ALM 上监控消息处理。

消息队列管理：在消息队列监控器中扩展和筛选场景详情
消息队列监控器现在显示特定于场景的属性，例如发送方（Sender）、接收方（Receiver）、应用消息类型（Application Message Type）、应用消息 ID（Application Message ID）和关联 ID（Correlation ID）。这些属性可以在消息表设置中启用，并用作筛选条件。

该功能允许您更高效地缩小消息范围，并快速识别问题条目（带有即时上下文），通过直接筛选相关消息缩短问题解决时间；支持更精确的故障排查和根因分析；通过仅显示关键消息来减少运营噪音；通过可见且可搜索的元数据提高审计与合规性；并简化运维、支持和开发团队之间的协作与交接。开始使用：管理存储在队列中的消息。

支持适配器类型的参数化映射
SAP Process Orchestration 导入到 SAP Integration Suite - Cloud Integration 的导入选项已扩展，支持在消息映射中为适配器类别定义参数，使您能够导入包含适配器类型参数的 PI/PO 消息映射；迁移工具和独立的消息映射导入现在可接受含有“Import”类型适配器参数类别的映射对象，从而保留适配器特定参数配置，减少迁移过程中的手动调整和返工，提高迁移映射的准确性，并通过保持与适配器参数相关的运行时行为来加速上手和故障排查。参见文档：从企业服务资源库（ESR）导入消息映射。

连接性更新

客户自管云连接器
Edge Integration Cell 运行时现在支持使用由客户自行管理的外部 SAP Cloud Connector，而非内置的 SAP 管理连接器，使组织可以将连接器部署在最符合其基础设施和安全需求的网络区域。这一功能增加了部署灵活性，提高了安全控制能力，支持额外的流量检查功能，并有助于满足基础设施与合规要求。参见配置步骤：为 Edge Integration Cell 配置外部 Cloud Connector。

此外，用户现在还可以通过 Edge 生命周期管理添加并初始化带有外部 Cloud Connector 的 Edge Node。参见：使用外部 Cloud Connector 初始化 Edge Node。

最新适配器
2026 年第一季度发布的适配器包括：

  • BambooHR 适配器：连接到 BambooHR，以访问员工、组织、休假等相关 API。
  • Zendesk 适配器：为工单、附件、请求、标签等多种实体提供完整的 CRUD 操作。该适配器用于接收和处理 SAP Integration Suite 中集成流程的数据，并通过基于 REST 的 API 与 Zendesk（外部）应用通信。
  • Microsoft 365 Outlook 适配器：通过改善通信并在两个平台之间自动化工作流，实现 Microsoft Outlook 与 SAP Integration Suite 的无缝集成。支持作为发送方和接收方适配器。🎥 观看演示。
  • OFTP2 适配器：使用 Odette 文件传输协议（OFTP）适配器，在 Edge Integration Cell 上与合作伙伴交换 EDI 和业务文件。
  • NFS 文件适配器：支持连接到 NFS 服务器，实现对 Edge Integration Cell 上文件的读写操作。🎥 观看演示。

数据中心可用性更新
SAP Integration Suite 新增的数据中心可用性选项包括：

  • 澳大利亚（悉尼）—SAP 基础设施
  • 日本（东京）和美国—SAP 托管基础设施
  • 英国—通过 Microsoft Azure 提供

进一步了解
想提前了解 SAP Integration Suite 在 2026 年的更多规划？观看该演示：

你最期待在2026年看到哪些关键创新？欢迎在评论区告诉我！

entry.title: Experience Modernized Enterprise Integration – Onsite!
entry.content:
After the latest wave of exciting announcements at SAP Sapphire 2026, you may still be buzzing from all the innovative technologies that lie ahead of us. But most of you probably want to see these innovations in action - interactive, hands-on, and with real people onsite.

Luckily, for everything related to Modernized Integration, we have something in store for you: the Integration Modernization AI Infodays in Germany. If you are based in Germany or located nearby, you can register for one of three onsite events and learn how to Accelerate Innovation with AI and Modern Integration.

Register here: https://events.sap.com/ai-driven-integration/en_us/home.html 

The Integration Modernization campaign turns inspiration into execution - showing how SAP Integration Suite operates as the control plane for Agent Governance: the backbone where APIs, events, identities, policies, and observability converge to safely orchestrate human and AI agents at scale.

We translate vision into actionable architecture by modernizing legacy middleware, establishing API- and event-first design principles, and operationalizing guardrails for AI and agent workloads.

Attendees will learn how to:

  • Understand new integration patterns such as Agent-to-System and Agent-to-Agent
  • Explain the MCP Reference Architecture
  • Use the MCP protocol to connect and govern agent capabilities in a policy-aware way
  • Easily consume APIs with consistent security, throttling, and lifecycle controls via SAP Integration Suite
  • Automate workflows (e.g., with n8n) to build event-driven, composable solutions - accelerating innovation while staying within enterprise guardrails
  • Understand identity setup across integration components (e.g., API keys and SAP IAS)

With SAP Integration Suite providing secure connectivity, policy enforcement, and end-to-end monitoring, you'll reduce technical debt, increase delivery velocity, and move decisively toward an autonomous enterprise.

After SAPPHIRE comes the opportunity to get your hands dirty - join us on the journey.

If you are interested please refer to this registration page to secure your spot: https://events.sap.com/ai-driven-integration/en_us/home.html

Stay tuned!

entry.title: Middleware Isn't Dead. It Grew Up.
entry.content:
The recent online conversation sparked by SAP's strategic investment in n8n has been energetic, and largely for the right reasons. The excitement is warranted. The investment is significant. But some of what's circulating online conflates a few things that are worth separating clearly — and some of the most important parts of the story are getting lost in the noise.

This post is my attempt to clear that up.

 

What the n8n partnership actually is

At SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP announced a strategic investment in n8n — the open-source workflow automation platform — as part of a multi-year commercial partnership with deep product alignment.

This is a genuine strategic move, and the reason is worth stating plainly: SAP didn't partner with n8n to acquire a tool. SAP partnered with n8n to gain access to a community. n8n brings 1.7 million active builders, 1,000+ app and system integrations, and a thriving ecosystem of workflows and templates built by people who were never traditional SAP developers. That's a different builder profile — technical generalists, citizen developers, automation engineers — and it's exactly the kind of expansion the SAP developer ecosystem has long needed. You no longer need to know ABAP or CDS views to build useful business automations.

For practitioners, what this means in practice is a visual, node-based workflow canvas embedded alongside the agent and app-building capabilities in Joule Studio, SAP's unified agent-building, app development, and automation environment within the SAP Business AI Platform. Builders who live in automation-first tools will feel immediately at home. The possibilities for connecting SAP agents to external services and building agentic workflows visually are genuinely compelling.

As we look to the future, expect SAP to continue to deepen this partnership. n8n's reach across the SAP landscape will grow, but more on that direction as it develops.

What the n8n partnership is not

Here is where some of the online commentary has gone sideways.

n8n in Joule Studio is a workflow automation and agent orchestration layer. SAP Integration Suite is an enterprise integration backbone. They solve fundamentally different problems, and treating one as a substitute for the other creates real architectural risk.

Integration Suite handles the things that have to work every time, at scale, under governance: enterprise-grade process integration, B2B EDI and trading partner management, full API lifecycle from design through monetization, hybrid and sovereign deployment through Edge Integration Cell, enterprise-grade event streaming, end-to-end error handling, SLA management, and audit trails for business-critical process flows. These are the scenarios where a visual workflow canvas is a complement, not a substitute.

The more useful frame is this: Joule Studio is where you build the agent, the app, or the workflow. Integration Suite is what lets those components reach every system, every process, and every partner across your enterprise — securely and at scale. The Joule Studio positioning is explicit about this: Integration Suite is named as one of the foundational layers that makes agents enterprise-ready. The two together are additive, and practitioners are better off for it.

The differing viewpoints in the online conversation are healthy

A range of perspectives has surfaced in these discussions — some overstating what n8n changes, some appropriately cautious about what it doesn't yet do. That kind of discourse sharpens everyone's thinking. But a few misconceptions keep appearing, and the most important one to address is the idea that n8n's arrival in Joule Studio resolves the PI/PO question.

It doesn't. Not yet, and not for most scenarios.

The PI/PO clock is real

SAP Process Integration and Process Orchestration reach end of standard maintenance in 2027, with extended maintenance available through 2030. That date is not moving.

The vast majority of PI/PO workloads are traditional integration scenarios — system-to-system message routing, B2B document exchange, ABAP proxy patterns, IDoc flows, RFC and JDBC lookups — that map directly and logically to SAP Integration Suite. These are not workflow automation scenarios. Migration tooling for these patterns exists today: the Migration Assessment tool is available on the free tier of Integration Suite and can give you a complete inventory and effort estimate of your PI/PO landscape without any commitment. The Migration Tool in Cloud Integration handles the conversion for the most common adapter patterns.

If you haven't started making a plan to migrate off PI/PO, it's not too late — but it's getting there. A large landscape with 300+ interfaces can take a year or more even with the best tooling. The time to start assessing is now, not when the maintenance window starts closing.

The good news: that migration is about to get meaningfully faster. SAP announced the PI/PO Migration Agent at Sapphire 2026, with general availability targeted for Q3 2026. This agent brings AI-driven automation to the medium and high complexity migrations that previously required the most specialist effort — the ones involving custom code, complex mappings, and logic that the existing Migration Tool couldn't handle automatically. For landscapes where migration friction has been the primary blocker, this agent compresses the execution phase significantly.

If you haven't started your assessment yet, there's no reason to wait. SAP's Migration Factory offers a free migration assessment to help you understand the scope, complexity, and effort of your PI/PO landscape before you commit to anything. Apply for your free assessment here.

The bigger picture

The n8n investment, the PI/PO Migration Agent, the MCP Gateway reaching general availability this quarter, the Joule conversational interface coming to Integration Suite in Q3 — these aren't separate announcements. They're part of a coherent direction: an integration platform that is becoming intelligent from the inside, connected to the entire enterprise, and accessible to a much broader builder community than ever before.

More tools. More flexibility. More paths to get your integration and automation landscape where it needs to be.

The middleware argument hasn't been killed. It's been answered — with options.

entry.title: A Big Leap Beyond C4C: Changes in Communication Systems & Authentication in Sales Cloud V2 - Part 1
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog explores the changes to the Communication System in SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2 (SSCV2) that lay the foundation for integrating with external systems, whether through direct API connectivity, middleware-mediated scenarios via SAP Integration Suite, or side-by-side extensibility on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). It also covers the authentication mechanisms available in SCV2 and how they compare and, in many ways, contrast with what SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C) offered.

Having worked in the SAP CX space for over 12 years across SAP C4C, SAP Field Service Management (FSM), SAP Customer Data Cloud (Gigya), and SAP Marketing Cloud, our recent implementation on SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2 gained some significant learnings, particularly around how the integration and extensibility model has fundamentally evolved from the C4C era.

The following abbreviations are used throughout this blog:

SAP C4C - SAP Cloud for Customer

SAP SSCV2 - SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2 (latest version of the product)

 

What is a Communication System?

In both SAP C4C and Sales/Service Cloud V2, a Communication System represents an external logical system instance, such as a SAP BTP subaccount, an ERP back-end, or a middleware platform that exchanges data with Sales Cloud. Think of it as the identity card you issue to a partner system before it can walk in the door.

 

SAP C4C Baseline — What We're Moving Away From

SAP Cloud for Customer's integration model has the authentication methods that support the administrators to have two choices:

  • Basic Authentication

  • SSL / X.509 Certificates

Why this matters now: Basic Authentication is being phased out across SAP's landscape. SAP SuccessFactors and SAP BTP have already announced sunset timelines for Basic Auth on their APIs. Organisations must migrate their integrations to OAuth 2.0 before this deadline to prevent disruptions.

 

SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2 Communication System — A Richer Model

SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2 provides a future-proof solution with multiple options to the SAP partners/customers.

When you create a Communication System in Sales Cloud V2 (via Settings → Integration → Communication Systems), you'll immediately notice it's structured around two distinct directions, each with its own authentication model.

 

 

Deleted Communication Systems

A key improvement in SAP SSCV2 is the ability to restore the Communication System after it is deleted.

An Administrator can delete a Communication System, and it is moved to the Deleted Communication Systems rather than being removed from the system entirely, unlike in SAP C4C.

Steps to Delete a Communication System

  1. Change the Status of the Communication System from Active to Inactive

  1. Click the Delete button

  1. Deleted Communication System visible in the section below:

  2. Remaining Days shows the number of days until the System removes it from the Deletion table in SAP SSCV2. Clicking the Undo button will make the Communication System Inactive

  3. Clicking the Delete button will permanently delete it from SAP SSCV2.

The retention time for the Deleted Communication System is 60 days. After 60 days, it is permanently deleted and cannot be recovered.

 

Authentication Methods: Comparison between SAP C4C and SAP SSCV2

The table below lists the authentication methods available for Inbound and Outbound connectivity with SAP SSCV2.

The ideal scenario would be to integrate SAP SSCV2 with SAP Integration Suite and use SAP BTP (Kyma or Cloud Foundry) for extensibility.

 

DirectionTypeSAP C4CSAP SSCV2Notable Changes

Inbound

Basic Authentication (Username / Password)

Business user controlled by Business Roles assigned & Technical User authentication.

Technical User authentication is controlled by the Technical Roles assigned. Clear separation between a Technical User and a business user

Improved access control on Technical User and Technical Roles in SAP SSCV2

Inbound

SSL Client Certificates

Key pair certificate created in C4C, middleware, or an external system.

Key pair certificate created in a middleware or an external system.

Remains the same with a different user experience in SAP SSCV2

Outbound

Basic Authentication (Username / Password)

A username and password provided by a middleware or an external system that has access to the respective API.

A username and password provided by a middleware or an external system that has access to the respective API.

Remains the same with a different user experience in SAP SSCV2

Outbound

SSL Certificate Authentication

SAP trusted, or a 3rd party trusted Key pair certificate downloadable from Outbound Communication from C4C

Keypair certificate created in BTP, middleware with a passphrase uploaded in SSCV2.

A key pair certificate can only be created in external systems and uploaded to SAP SSCV2

Outbound

OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials Grant

NA

The Client fetches the access token and passes the token in the subsequent step to get access to the resources

New feature in SAP SSCV2

Outbound

OAuth 2.0 SAML Bearer Assertion

NA

Principal propagation is achieved through the OAuth2 SAML Bearer assertion flow. The sender app uses a custom identity provider that also acts as a SAML token issuer.

New feature in SAP SSCV2

Outbound

OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange

NA

Get a new token for an existing token with a different scope and audience, and pass the new token to access the resources.

New feature in SAP SSCV2



Recommendation: Don't use the Basic authentication method in productive tenants for security reasons

How to choose the right Authentication method

Choosing the right authentication method in SAP SSCV2 is not purely a technical decision; it also has implications for security governance, audit compliance, and the operational overhead your basis and integration teams will carry throughout the life of the implementation. The right choice depends on three major things:

  • Who or what is calling SAP SSCV2

  • Whether a logged-in user's identity needs to be traceable in the system

  • Whether middleware sits between the caller and SAP SSCV2.

The table below summarises the recommendations based on our experience by scenario:

 

Scenario

Recommended Authentication Method

Pros

Cons

Direct API Call using Basic Auth, where no possibility of maintaining the Certificate or OAuth

Basic Auth (Technical User/Technical Role)

  • Familiar, quick to set up, and works without any additional infrastructure.

  • Appropriate for development sandboxes and early-phase integration testing

  • SAP SSCV2 provides better control through features such as Technical User and Technical Roles, which provide granular access to APIs.

  • Credentials are long-lived, shared between systems, and must be rotated manually, all of which introduces risk as the integration landscape grows.

  • Basic Authentication, in most cases, should be treated as a starting point, not a target state.

For integrations running through SAP Cloud Integration

Client Certificate Authentication for Inbound 

& 

Client Certificate Authentication or OAuth 2.0 – Client Credentials for Outbound

  • The Communication System in SCV2 is configured with the Integration Suite tenant's certificate on the inbound side.

  • SAP SSCV2 uses the BTP-generated certificate from the service instance for outbound calls into Integration Suite iFlows

  • Similar to integration patterns with SAP C4C

  • The certificate validity period is visible and auditable.

  • Initial Setup is required, and technical knowledge of certificate work is required

 

For SAP BTP side-by-side extensions

OAuth 2.0 with SAML Bearer Assertion

  • Passes the context of the logged-in user from and to SAP SSCV2. This is a pattern that enabled Principal Propagation

  • This is a new feature that was not available with C4C

  • An application embedded in SAP SSCV2 as a Mashup that runs in SAP BTP (created from SAP Build or apps running in Cloud Foundry or Kyma) will capture the actual logged-in user in the change logs in SAP SSCV2 rather than a generic technical user.

  • Since the logged-in user context is being passed, the logged-in user Business Role must have sufficient access to the API calls made in the application. Business Role must be managed in such a way

  • SAP Cloud Identity Service is required as a prerequisite.

  • The user should be available in the BTP subaccount has the necessary roles in BTP if the application is calling an integration flow.

 

Server-Server Calls (Ex: Custom Entity Service, Direct Non-SAP integrations)

OAuth 2.0 – Client Credentials

  • This is used to access an external service directly from the backend of Sales/Service Cloud V2 by using a dedicated Client ID.

  • Scopes can be configured to restrict what that token can access, which is especially valuable when integrating with systems outside the SAP landscape.

  • User context is not passed to the external service

 

In practice, the final choice of authentication method will be shaped by factors specific to each client's landscape and internal security policies.

Key considerations include:

  • Custom Identity Provider (IdP) availability

  • Enterprise security standards

  • Certificate lifecycle management

  • Existing integration platform capabilities

 

In my next blog, I will walk through the step-by-step configuration of the recommended authentication methods in SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2, covering Basic Auth using a Technical User, Client Certificate setup for SAP Integration Suite and OAuth 2.0 SAML Bearer Assertion for SAP BTP side-by-side extensions.

Thanks, 

Adi

entry.title: What We Learned Hosting a SAP Document AI Workshop
entry.content:
Introduction

Some weeks ago we had a hands-on event focused on SAP Document AI and enterprise document automation. We had planned it to be just a technical workshop, yet the attendees wasted no time in telling us all kinds of complications they were experiencing at the time. Soon, we were talking about operational processes, workflow complexity, and the increasing importance of AI in enterprise environments.

When we were planning the workshop we did not just want to show people a product. Our goal was to create a space where participants could engage directly with actual document processing situations, as well as investigate automation options that might enhance their routine workflows. I mean, you can't have an opinion about something you haven't used or experienced yourself.

During the session attendees had the opportunity to experiment with document classification, extraction models, workflow routing and automation processes related to larger SAP workflows.

As the Hackathon went on, the conversations naturally shifted toward some of the operational challenges many companies are currently facing. Although SAP Document AI was the focus, there was also room for repetitive manual work, fragmented validation processes, inconsistent document formats, and the difficulty of scaling document-heavy operations across teams and business units.

 

What the workshop was about

The workshop included product exploration and practical activities, simulating real enterprise scenarios. Rather than merely attend presentations or demos, participants were able to directly engage with workflows, classifiers, extraction schemas, and automation processes.

The event provided participants with insights into how SAP Document AI can support invoicing processes, free-form documents, workflow routing, and document classification.

 

One exercise that participants seemed to find particularly useful was the ROI calculator we brought to the workshop. While most of the day focused on workflows and automation scenarios, the calculator helped ground those discussions in something every organization cares about: business impact. It gave attendees a way to estimate how document automation could affect their own operations and helped move the conversation from technical possibilities to practical outcomes.

I think we were really taken aback by the diversity of perspectives in the room. People came from different industries and business environments, and brought very different operational realities and use cases to the discussion.

We were surprised that many of the issues being discussed were incredibly similar. Within all industries, companies had a lot of the same challenges around managing documents, approvals, integrations and keeping processes consistent. 

While working on practical document-processing scenarios, it became evident that the challenges most companies face go far beyond document extraction. As operations expand across multiple departments and teams, many organizations are still faced with fragmented workflows, repetitive manual validations, inconsistent document formats and processes that are becoming harder to manage.

And the workshop also reinforced something we often see when working with SAP technologies: real conversations happen once people can interact directly with a solution. The hackathon became much more practical and grounded after participants started to set up schemas, test classifiers and experiment with workflows. Rather than considering AI as something far away or theoretical, attendees began to consider how these solutions could realistically be integrated into their own operational environments.

Another important conclusion we arrived at was the importance of integration for successful AI adoption. Precise extraction models are crucial, no one will deny that, but they are just one part of the process. Companies also require workflows capable of adapting to operational changes, handling variations between document types, and collaborating effectively over time with existing systems.

Some Reflections

One of the most gratifying aspects of the workshop was to look back and see how quickly participants started to relate the exercises to their own organizations. Throughout the day the conversation evolved easily from examples under discussion to real business processes, internal priorities and potential next steps. Watching that transition in real time was a reminder of how the value of these sessions often goes far beyond the tech itself.

The workshop was also a great opportunity for us to hear directly from organizations that are facing different operational challenges, and to get a better understanding of how they are approaching document automation and AI adoption within their SAP landscapes.

Some of the most valuable insights came not only from the practical tasks but from the conversations that took place around them. The people who came had different perspectives, experiences and business contexts and we learnt from each other. The workshop was a valuable experience for all involved. As for me, I’m already looking forward to the next one, whether that’s as a host, a participant, or simply someone eager to keep learning.

entry.title: Configuring OAuth Credentials for SAP SuccessFactors – A Step by Step Guide
entry.content:
Hello All,

Greetings for the Day!!

Basic Authentication is being phased out for SuccessFactors. If your standard or custom SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) interfaces still rely on Basic Authentication, now is the time to transition to OAuth2.0.

Even though the SAP documentation regarding OAuth2 SAML is comprehensive enough, this article will guide you practically through the setup. It covers the end-to-end configuration across both CPI and SuccessFactors, including generation of certificates and registering your credentials.

This article outlines the exact methodology I utilize during the project implementations. It emphasizes the proper sequence of operations within both systems to ensure you can activate OAuth seamlessly, eliminating the need for trial and error. The steps are straightforward, reproducible, and perfect for distributing among your project members.

Prerequisites

  • Access to your SAP CPI tenant with permissions for Monitor → Keystore and Security Material.
  • Administrator access in SuccessFactors to manage OAuth2 Client Applications
  • SuccessFactors Company ID along with API/Data Center URL
  • A dedicated technical/API user in SuccessFactors

Architecture Overview

From a high level perspective, here is how the OAuth flow functions:

  1. CPI creates a key pair (the certificate);
  2. The certificate is then registered in the SuccessFactors by setting up an OAuth2 client mapped to a technical user;
  3. SuccessFactors generates an API Key specific to this client application.
  4. In CPI, an OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion credential is configured using the client parameters;
  5. Lastly, the iFlow leverages this credential to obtain access tokens, enabling secure communication with SuccessFactors APIs.

Part A - CPI: Create the Key Pair (Steps 1 - 4)

Step 1: Log in to your SAP CPI environment and navigate to Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Key store.

Step 2: Click Create → Key Pair.

Step 3: Populate the Key Pair parameters:

  • Alias: Choose a recognizable name for your setup (e.g., successfactorsoAuth).
  • Common Name (CN): Enter the exact SuccessFactors technical user ID.
  • Country/Region: Input your location code, such as IN (India).
  • Validity: set a sufficient validity period (don’t keep default short dates)

Step 4: Hit Create, then choose Download Certificate. You will need this certificate for the SuccessFactors configuration.

Tip: Keep a secure record of both the Alias and the CN, as you will need to reference them later during the CPI OAuth setup.

Part B - Success Factors: Register the OAuth Client (Steps 5 - 9)

Step 5: Sign into Success Factors and use the search tool to find Manage OAuth2 Client Applications.

Step 6: Click Register Client Application.

 

 

 

 

Step 7: Enter application details:

  • Application Name: You can give any meaningful name for your architechture.
  • Application URL: your middleware base URL (up to *.ondemand.com)
  • Check the box for Bind to Users
  • In User IDs section, provide the SF technical user (this must exactly match the CN you used in CPI)

Now, paste the CPI certificate:

  1. Open the downloaded certificate file using a basic text editor (like Notepad).
  2. Copy only the text situated between
    -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- and -----END CERTIFICATE-----
  3. Paste this string into X.509 Certificate field.

Click Register.

Step 8: Select View next to the newly created application to see its details.

Step 9: Copy the generated API Key and store it safely. This will be required for the CPI configuration.

Common pitfall: If the certificate block includes the BEGIN/END CERTIFICATE lines or extra white space, registration can fail. Only paste the primary block of text.

Part C - CPI: Create OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion Credential (Steps 10 - 12)

Step 10: Return to CPI and navigate to Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Security Material.

Step 11: Go to Create → OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion.

Step 12: Create a new entry with the following details:

  • Name: An identifiable title (e.g., SF_OAuth_SAML_Prod)
  • Audience: www.successfactors.com
  • Token Service URL: your API host + /oauth/token
    (For instance: https://api4.successfactors.com/oauth/token - make sure use your specific Data Center host).
  • Target System Type: Select SuccessFactors.
  • Company ID: your SF Company ID.
  • User ID: Select Key Pair Common Name (CN).
  • Key Pair Alias: the alias you created (e.g., successfactorsoAuth).
  • Client Key: The API key you retrieved from SuccessFactors.
  • Deploy.

 

Notes: 

  • The User ID must match the user you bound in SuccessFactors.
  • The Key Pair Alias must be exactly the CPI keystore alias.
  • The Token Service URL must be your DC’s API endpoint with /oauth/token.

Part D - Test with a Simple iFlow (Steps 13 - 15)

Step 13: Build a basic test iFlow that calls a simple SuccessFactors API (e.g., a GET on FOCompany or User with minimal fields).

Step 14: In the receiver adaptor settings, apply the following:
Authentication: Set to OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion.
Select the credential you created above.

Step 15: Turn HTTP Session Reuse = On Exchange (important).
This minimizes token overhead and prevents intermittent auth failures.

Deploy and execute the test. You should receive a valid response from Success Factors.

Troubleshooting (what usually goes wrong)

401/403 errors:

  • Review your Audience and Token URL (spelling mistakes or incorrect DC hosts happen frequently).
  • Verify that HTTP Session Reuse is turned on in the adapter settings.
  • Double-check the Company ID and User ID in the CPI credential.

Invalid certificate / client errors:

  • Re‑paste only the certificate body exclusively (without BEGIN/END lines).
  • Check the CPI Keystore to make sure the Key Pair hasn't passed its expiration date.

Wrong user context:

  • The User ID applied in “Bind to Users” must be the same user used as CN in the CPI Key Pair and User ID in CPI OAuth credential.

Token URL not reachable:

  • Confirm if the API host is correct for your data center.
  • Ensure there are no proxies/firewalls that might be restricting outgoing connections from CPI to SF.

Best Practices (My Project Recommendations)

  1. Make use of Technical User in SF for API interactions rather than relying on personal or administrator accounts;
  2. Monitor certificate validity – set up calendar reminders 30–45 days before expiry.
  3. Store API Keys securely; refrain from sharing them in casual chats or open documents.
  4. Enable HTTP Session Reuse for the SuccessFactors adapters.
  5. Start with a minimal test iFlow before applying changes to your live business integrations.
  6. Review OAuth clients in SF quarterly; remove unused clients and rotate certs periodically.
  7. Use CPI Monitoring & Traces for root cause; they’re incredibly valuable for OAuth issues.

Conclusion

If you have followed the above mentioned instructions generating the key pair in CPI, OAuth client in SuccessFactors, credential in CPI, and a small test iFlow you’re set for a clean migration from Basic Auth to OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion.

The vast majority of errors I encounter are configuration typos or missed details (wrong token URL, incorrect User ID/CN mapping, certificate pasted incorrectly, or HTTP Session Reuse left off). Once those are correct, the flow is very stable.

Implementing this architecture ensures your integrations remain aligned with SAP’s roadmap, preventing you from unexpected disruptions when Basic Authentication is officially retired.

Best Regards,
Rasesh Thakkar.

entry.title: A Practical Approach to Automating External Parameter Changes in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

As SAP Integration Suite developers, we regularly need to adjust external parameter values throughout a project’s lifecycle. This is more than a good practice: it helps ensure that interfaces remain tenant-agnostic and easier to maintain across environments.

In many projects, these adjustments are only required during transport between environments. In more complex landscapes, however, what appears to be a simple task can quickly become a major operational challenge for any CPI specialist.

 

Example Scenario

Imagine you have built multiple interfaces and your project also includes SAP standard packages such as Cell and Gene Therapy Orchestration (CGTO) or SAP Digital Manufacturing (DM). In addition, SAP Event Mesh is being used as the event broker for CGTO.

Although this setup may appear straightforward, complications often arise at the architectural level. For reasons such as budget constraints or time pressure, the DEV and QA landscapes in this example include not only the standard systems but also hotfix systems dedicated to specific testing activities.

 

 

 

In the DEV scenario described above, SAP Integration Suite acts as the central backbone for all communication flows. While there may be two instances of SAP CGTO, two of SAP S/4HANA, and so on, there is only one SAP Integration Suite tenant responsible for connecting these systems correctly.

Once this landscape is defined, SAP Integration Suite must be adjusted accordingly to ensure that all communication channels function correctly. This becomes especially critical when the hotfix testing window is extremely limited and the parallel system remains offline. These adjustments can involve hundreds or even thousands of configuration values, making it unrealistic to update them manually within such a short period without a significant risk of error.

To illustrate the scale of the challenge, nearly 200 external parameters may be affected in a scenario like this, for example, SAP clients, queue paths, and system endpoints.

 

Solution Overview

A practical way to address this challenge is to leverage SAP Integration Suite itself, more specifically, the integration content available through SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

This approach makes it possible to extract the affected external parameters, maintain them in a spreadsheet, save them as a CSV file, and submit the update through an external tool such as Postman. The effort of preparing the configuration files is done only once; after that, switching between environment-specific configurations can be completed in just a few minutes by uploading the appropriate file.

 

Soft Configurations Framework

The concept is straightforward: this interface provides a controlled way to manage external parameters in SAP Integration Suite through an external client such as Postman.

 The goal is to reduce manual configuration effort across multiple environments while improving traceability and lowering the risk of human error.

 

How the Solution Works

Below are the iFlows included in this solution scope.

Get iFlows

This iFlow retrieves the external parameters of iFlows within the selected SAP Integration Suite packages and returns the result in either JSON or CSV format.

Options:

  • ignorePackages

Because of the large number of packages involved, this option was created to exclude any packages that fall outside the scope of the hotfix, for example, sandbox packages used for testing or other interfaces that are completely unrelated to the hotfix process.

  • returnPackage

If you only need to query a specific package, this option is much simpler, and significantly faster, than downloading every package available on the tenant.

  • Csv

By default, the interface returns the results in JSON format, which is convenient for inspection and integration. For SAP standard packages or large-scale parameter updates, however, CSV is often the most practical option because it allows all parameters to be reviewed and maintained efficiently in Microsoft Excel.

  • csvDelimiter

The user can also select the delimiter to be used in the CSV output.

 

Update Soft Config

This iFlow reads a JSON or CSV payload and updates the corresponding iFlows within the defined scope. It returns a full JSON report in Postman showing the affected external parameters, the previous and updated values, and the response status for each change.

Options:

  • Email

Because a full JSON report of the affected external parameters is generated, the interface can also send this report to a designated email inbox. This is particularly useful for traceability and audit purposes.

  • Deploy

This option allows the affected iflows to have their changes saved or saved and deployed.

 

iFlow Design and Processing Logic

 

Get All iFlows

Bellow are all the steps for this interface development.

Integration Process

  • Headers – Content Modifier: Reads all header parameters sent by a third-party application, such as Postman.
  • Read CSV Header – Groovy Script: Checks whether a CSV file has been requested.
  • Get Packages – Process Call: Invokes the local Get Packages process.

Get Packages

  • Get Packages – Request Reply: Retrieves all packages from SAP Integration Suite for the specified tenant (defined directly in the URL).
  • Read Packages – Groovy Script: Processes the returned payload and performs the following actions:
    • If returnPackage is enabled, filters the payload based on the packages specified in the client request.
    • If ignorePackages is enabled, returns all packages except those specified in the client request.
    • Creates a clean internal JSON structure containing the package ID, Name, and ShortText.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

def slim = results.collect { r ->
        [
            Id        : r?.Id ?: "",
            Name      : r?.Name ?: "",
            ShortText : r?.ShortText ?: ""
        ]
}
  • Total Packages – Router: If no packages are returned, the process terminates.
  • Get Integrations – Looping Process Call: Invokes the Get Artifacts from Package process for each package.

 

Get Artifacts from Package

  • Header – Content Modifier: Resets the current iFlow count.
  • Get Actual Package – Groovy Script: Since the next step requires the package ID for the request, this script retrieves the current package ID from the internal JSON message.
  • Get Artifacts from Package – Request Reply: Retrieves all iFlows associated with the selected package ID.
  • Get iFlows – Groovy Script: Processes the returned payload and appends the iFlow ID, Name, and ShortText, along with the current package ID, to the internal JSON message.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

found = false

jsonAll.each { item ->

	if ((item instanceof Map) && String.valueOf(item.Id) == targetPackage) 
	{
		item.Iflows = slim
		found = true
	}
}
  • Empty? – Router: If no iFlows are returned, the process ends and proceeds to the next package.
  • Get Soft Configurations – Looping Process Call: Invokes the local Get Soft Configurations process.

 

Get Soft configurations

  • Get iFlow – Groovy Script: For the package currently in scope, retrieves each iFlow ID required to request its soft configurations in the next step.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

if(resultValue)
{      
	message.setProperty("iflowID", resultValue)
	message.setProperty("currentCountIflow", targetIflowIx + 1)
}
  • Get iFlow Soft Configs – Request Reply: Retrieves all soft configurations for each iFlow.
  • Get Soft Configs and Update – Groovy Script: Processes the retrieved information by cleaning the message, filtering out soft configurations defined directly within the iFlow, and appending the remaining entries to the internal JSON message.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

Message processData(Message message) {

    def body       = message.getBody(String) ?: '{}'
    def parsed     = new JsonSlurper().parseText(body)
    def results    = parsed?.d?.results ?: []

    def slim = results.collect {
        [
            Parameter   : it?.ParameterKey   ?: "",
            Value       : it?.ParameterValue ?: ""
            //Description : it?.Description    ?: ""
        ]
    }

    ...

    def pkg = jsonAll.find { it?.Id == packageID }

    // Ignore list
    def ignoreList = ignore.split(",")
                        .collect { it.trim() }
                        .findAll { it }

    // remove from list
    slim = slim.findAll { r -> !(r?.Parameter in ignoreList) }    

    if (pkg?.Iflows) {
        // Find the iflow
        def flow = pkg.Iflows.find { it?.IdIflow == iflowID }
        if (flow) {
            flow.SoftConfig = slim
        }
    }
...
}

 

  Integration Process

  • Return Message – Content Modifier: Loads the internal JSON message into the message body.
  • isCSV? – Router: Checks whether a CSV output was requested. If not, the process ends and returns the internal JSON message.
  • CSV Convert – Groovy Script: Converts the JSON message into CSV format using the specified delimiter.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

// HEADER
    StringBuilder csv = new StringBuilder()
    csv.append(["PackageID","PackageName","IdIflow","NameIflow","Parameter","Value"].join(separator))
       .append(nl)

    // Clear escapes
    def esc = { v ->
        if (v == null) return ""
        def s = v.toString()
        def needsQuotes = s.contains(separator) || s.contains('"') || s.contains('\n') || s.contains('\r')
        s = s.replace('"', '""')
        needsQuotes ? "\"${s}\"" : s
    }

    // packages
    json.each { pkg ->
        def pkgId   = pkg.Id
        def pkgName = pkg.Name
        def iflows  = pkg.Iflows ?: []

        // iFlows
        iflows.each { iflow ->
            def iflowId   = iflow.IdIflow
            def iflowName = iflow.NameIflow
